The Grand Master Legend

Seven
Confirmed

Seven Grand Masters across eight centuries. One position remains open. The app is counting every win.

👑 2,500 verified wins  ·  The threshold for the Eighth
#1 of 7
Soraya al-Zafar
821 AD · Persia
3200W · 0L

Soraya al-Zafar played along the Silk Road trading routes across Persia for over six decades, defeating every challenger she met. The first player in recorded history to achieve 3,000 verified wins. She recorded no verified loss in her entire career.

✍️ The Null ✍️ The Still Hand ✍️ The Silk Road
View Fight Card →
#2 of 7
Giriama Kenga
917 AD · Mombasa, East Africa
2774W · 12L

A coastal East African trader and community elder who publicly acknowledged each of her twelve losses by name. She believed the game was a form of conversation between bodies — losing was simply receiving information you had not yet understood.

✍️ Patient Observation ✍️ Forest Spirit ✍️ The Long Wait
View Fight Card →
#3 of 7
Kofi Diabaté
1193 · Mali Empire
2900W · 4L

A griot-warrior of the Mali Empire who embedded the game within musical and oral traditions. The only Grand Master known to have taught the game publicly and systematically. His four losses are the most of any Grand Master above rank three — each opponent is named in the records he left behind.

✍️ The Pendulum ✍️ The Drum Beat ✍️ The Seven Hands
View Fight Card →
#4 of 7
Velu Nair
1441 · Indian Ocean Trade Routes
2100W · 8L

A navigator and spice trader who played across the Indian Ocean network. The only Grand Master documented to have competed at sea. He trained with his eyes closed for the last thirty years of his career, claiming that sight introduced predictability.

✍️ Eyes-Closed Silk Rotation ✍️ The Navigator ✍️ The Open Palm
View Fight Card →
#5 of 7
Little Big Ben
1717 · London, England
1920W · 1L

Known only as Little Big Ben — his given name was never recorded. A child of approximately nine or ten years old who appeared on the banks of the Thames and defeated every adult challenger for three years. He disappeared from record in 1720. His single loss was to a dockworker who distracted him with a coin.

✍️ The Tower ✍️ Clockwork ✍️ The Strike
View Fight Card →
#6 of 7
Hanako Mori
1874 · Osaka, Japan
3100W · 6L

The most recent confirmed Grand Master. A former textile worker who received her certification in 1961 at age 87. She played through two world wars, claiming the game was the only form of communication that transcended language and conflict.

✍️ Pressure-Field Sensing ✍️ The Seamstress ✍️ Hearing the Arm Think
View Fight Card →
#7 of 7
Marcus Cole
1945 · New York City, USA
2600W · 9L

A former amateur boxer from Harlem who discovered the game in a Brooklyn shipyard in 1945. He was the first player to theorise the spatial relationship between stance width and grip range, documenting his findings in a notebook that circulated in New York athletic circles for decades.

✍️ The Reach ✍️ Street Geometry ✍️ The Freeze
View Fight Card →
